Q1: I usually organized my study routine around listening to lectures and recorded materials, since I learn best through audio. I'd set aside about 15 hours a week, often in the evenings after work or other responsibilities. I tried to create a consistent schedule, but honestly, my anxiety sometimes made it hard to stick to plans perfectly. I found that having a routine helped me feel more in control, even when things got overwhelming.
Q2: My pattern varied quite a bit depending on deadlines and how anxious I was feeling that week. I aimed for regularity, but life at 27 with various responsibilities doesn't always cooperate. When assignments were due, I'd intensify my study sessions significantly. The CBT sessions I attended actually helped me recognize when I was spiraling and get back on track with my schedule.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new lecture recordings or seeing if there were discussion posts I needed to respond to. Sometimes my anxiety would actually push me to log in just to make sure I hadn't missed anything important. I'd also log in when I got notifications about upcoming deadlines or when I wanted to review materials before working on assignments.
Q4: Yes, after a few weeks I got into a rhythm of logging in most mornings to check announcements and then again in the evening for actual studying. It became almost automatic, like checking email. The discussion forums helped because I felt a sense of connection with my peers, which made me want to come back. Having those 22 peer interactions throughout the course really helped normalize the habit.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because of my auditory learning style. I watched over 240 minutes of video content, often replaying sections to really absorb the material. The discussion forums were also valuable because hearing different perspectives helped concepts click for me. When peers explained things in their own words, it was like getting the lecture content reinforced from another angle.
Q6: I'll admit I sometimes skimmed through reading materials or downloaded them with good intentions but didn't always get through them thoroughly. Text-heavy resources just don't stick with me the way audio does. I also delayed some of the supplementary materials when my anxiety was high because I'd prioritize the core requirements first to feel less overwhelmed.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to my assignments or exams. If I could listen to something and immediately apply it, that was gold. The discussion posts felt worthwhile because I was contributing to a community and learning from others simultaneously. Anything that reduced my anxiety about upcoming assessments was automatically valuable to me.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure, which was actually a double-edged sword. On one hand, the lower stakes meant less anxiety. On the other hand, when my stress was high, optional things were the first to get cut from my schedule. Required activities had a built-in motivation that optional ones lacked, even when the optional content was genuinely interesting.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up in the LMS. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my commute or while doing chores. I also took handwritten notes while listening because writing helps me process auditory information. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with friends outside the platform, which was incredibly helpful for my understanding.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged but the LMS wouldn't know it. I'd pause a video, spend twenty minutes thinking through a concept, maybe even talk it through out loud to myself. I also used external resources like YouTube videos or podcasts on related topics to supplement my learning. My best studying often happened away from the screen entirely.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd access a lecture video once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never open it on the LMS again. My notes became my primary study material for exam prep. I'd listen to my voice recordings of key concepts repeatedly, which was my own creation, not something the LMS could track. This approach worked really well for my learning style.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my anxiety spiked and everything felt harder. Midway through the course, I had some personal responsibilities pile up, and even logging in felt overwhelming. The surprising thing was that the CBT sessions helped me recognize these patterns and develop coping strategies. My resilience kicked in, and I managed to push through those difficult periods.
Q13: Workload and competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. At 27, I'm juggling more than just coursework, and sometimes that meant my engagement looked lower even when I was still committed. Fatigue was real—there were nights I'd log in, stare at the screen, and realize I wasn't absorbing anything. Confusion sometimes made me avoid certain materials until I felt mentally prepared to tackle them.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd click through materials almost mechanically, especially when my anxiety was high. I'd watch a video but realize at the end that I'd been worrying about something else the whole time. It's frustrating because the activity looks the same in the data, but the actual learning wasn't happening. I learned to recognize this and would come back later when I could truly focus.
Q15: I definitely postponed things, usually the activities that felt most challenging or anxiety-inducing. Written assignments were harder for me than audio-based learning, so I'd delay starting them. Even knowing something was important, my brain would find reasons to avoid it. The CBT techniques helped me break this pattern by tackling tasks in smaller chunks rather than facing the whole thing at once.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me—maybe too much so. I'd often have bursts of intense activity right before something was due. The LMS probably shows this pattern clearly in my login frequency. While this approach got the work done, it also spiked my anxiety. I tried to balance this by starting earlier, but old habits are hard to break completely.
Q17: Feedback had a significant impact on my engagement. Positive feedback boosted my confidence and made me want to engage more. Constructive criticism was harder to receive because of my anxiety, but my resilience helped me use it productively. I'd often revisit materials after getting feedback to understand where I went wrong. Good feedback made the whole course feel more like a dialogue.
Q18: The course structure was generally helpful—having clear modules and deadlines gave me a framework to work within. The discussion forums being integrated helped me stay connected with peers, which I needed. However, when too many things were due at once, the structure felt suffocating rather than supportive. A more spaced-out schedule would have helped manage my anxiety better.
Q19: The availability of video content made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. Having discussion forums where I could interact with peers also helped. What made it harder was when important information was buried in long text documents—I'd sometimes miss things because reading isn't my strongest mode. More audio options for announcements would have been helpful.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying but struggling to produce work that meets their own standards. High anxiety might cause someone to research extensively but freeze when it comes to actually submitting. It could also be someone who's engaging with the content but facing external barriers to completing assignments. The activity shows effort, even if the output doesn't match.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some degree. My exam score and assignment scores were decent, but my LMS engagement level was rated low. I was learning effectively through downloaded materials, offline notes, and peer discussions that didn't show up in the system. The LMS only captures one slice of how I actually engaged with the course content.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're most active, what you click on, how long you spend on things. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if I'm deeply focused or just letting a video play in the background. They miss all the offline work, the thinking, the discussions with friends, the anxiety that affects how I process information.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely connecting with the material and caring about understanding it, not just completing tasks. For me, it's when I find myself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when I want to discuss ideas with others. It's feeling invested in my own learning journey, even when anxiety makes it difficult. True engagement survives the hard moments.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the CBT sessions helped me manage my anxiety and stay engaged despite internal struggles. It wouldn't capture the resilience I had to build to keep going when things got overwhelming. It also misses how my auditory learning style meant I was absorbing information in ways that didn't register as platform activity. My engagement was real, even when it was invisible.
